adolescent_relevance: Classic HD typically manifests in adulthood, though juvenile forms exist. GENARCH uses HD to illustrate genetic causation and ethical constraints: no individual-level prediction or counseling content is provided.

exposure_modifiers.mechanism_hypothesis: Sleep patterns can affect neurocognitive function broadly; relationships to HD progression are complex and not interpreted as causal guidance.
Stress can influence neuroendocrine and mental health pathways; effects on HD course are complex and context-dependent.
Physical activity relates to general health and neurocognitive outcomes; GENARCH does not treat this as medical advice for HD.

heritability_estimate.source: Monogenic causal architecture (repeat expansion in HTT)

genetic_architecture.prs_notes: PRS is not applicable as a primary risk model for monogenic HD. GENARCH does not provide individual predictions.

top_loci.ancestry_composition: Repeat expansion distribution varies by population history
top_loci.evidence: CAG repeat expansion in HTT is the causal mechanism for HD.

name: Huntington Disease

population_equity.data_gaps: Environmental modifier evidence is limited and often observational; GENARCH emphasizes uncertainty.
population_equity.gwas_ancestry_breakdown: HD research representation differs by geography; repeat expansion prevalence varies by population history.
population_equity.transferability_notes: Most mechanistic evidence is not ancestry-specific, but care access and measurement context differ across settings.

references.title: Huntington disease: genetics and molecular pathogenesis (review)
Sleep duration and health outcomes: overview of population evidence (review)
Psychosocial stress and health outcomes: mechanisms and evidence (review)
WHO guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour

summary:
Huntington disease (HD) is a hereditary neurodegenerative disorder caused by a CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene. The expanded polyglutamine tract leads to toxic protein effects and progressive neuronal dysfunction, often affecting movement, cognition, and behavior. While the genetic cause is the primary determinant of disease, population-level outcomes can still be shaped by supportive care access and broader environmental context.

GENARCH presents HD as an educational example of monogenic architecture with clear molecular causation. It does not provide individualized prediction, age-of-onset estimation, or clinical recommendations.
